Γ1, Γ2, Γ3 ⊆ LV and φ, ψ, ξ ∈ LV, for some PL LV. Prove the following
properties of logical implication.
a) If Γ1 |= φ and Γ2 |= ψ, then Γ1 ∪ Γ2 |= φ ∧ ψ.

Assume Γ1 |= φ and Γ2 |= ψ. This means that every interpretation that satisfies Γ1 also satisfies φ, and every interpretation that satisfies Γ2 also satisfies ψ. Let I be an interpretation that satisfies Γ1 ∪ Γ2. Then I satisfies both Γ1 and Γ2. Since I satisfies Γ1, I satisfies φ. Since I satisfies Γ2, I satisfies ψ. Therefore, I satisfies φ ∧ ψ.

Since I was chosen arbitrarily, this means that every interpretation that satisfies Γ1 ∪ Γ2 also satisfies φ ∧ ψ. Therefore, Γ1 ∪ Γ2 |= φ ∧ ψ, which completes the proof.

In what follows, Γ1, Γ2, Γ3 ⊆ LV and φ, ψ, ξ ∈ LV, for some PL LV. Prove the following
properties of logical implication
 If Γ1 ∪ {φ} |= ψ, then Γ1 |= φ ⇒ ψ

Assume that Γ1 ∪ {φ} |= ψ. This means that every interpretation that satisfies Γ1 and φ also satisfies ψ. We want to show that Γ1 |= φ ⇒ ψ, which means that every interpretation that satisfies Γ1 and φ also satisfies Γ1 ⇒ ψ.

Let I be an interpretation that satisfies Γ1 and φ. We need to show that I satisfies Γ1 ⇒ ψ. This means that if J is any interpretation that satisfies Γ1, then J also satisfies ψ.

Assume that J is an interpretation that satisfies Γ1. We need to show that J satisfies ψ. Since I satisfies Γ1 and φ, it also satisfies Γ1. Since Γ1 ⊆ Γ1 ∪ {φ}, J also satisfies Γ1 ∪ {φ}. Therefore, I and J satisfy the same set of formulas, and since Γ1 ∪ {φ} |= ψ, we have that I satisfies ψ. 

But since I and J were both arbitrary interpretations that satisfy Γ1 and φ, and since both satisfy ψ, we conclude that every interpretation that satisfies Γ1 and φ also satisfies Γ1 ⇒ ψ. Therefore, Γ1 |= φ ⇒ ψ, which completes the proof.

Using Wang’s algorithm, determine whether
a) {P ⇒ Q} |= ((P ⇒ ¬Q) ⇒ ¬P).

We will use Wang's algorithm to determine whether {P ⇒ Q} |= ((P ⇒ ¬Q) ⇒ ¬P).

Step 1: Write the premises and conclusion in the form of a sequent.
{P ⇒ Q} ⇒ ((P ⇒ ¬Q) ⇒ ¬P)

Step 2: Negate the conclusion and convert the sequent to conjunctive normal form (CNF).
{P ⇒ Q}, P ⇒ ¬Q, P

Step 3: Apply resolution to the CNF until either an empty clause (indicating validity) or a fixed point (indicating invalidity) is reached.

1. {P ⇒ Q}, P ⇒ ¬Q, P   (Premises in CNF)
2. {¬P, Q}, P ⇒ ¬Q, P   (Resolution on 1st and 2nd premises, generating a new clause)
3. {¬P, Q}, {¬P}, P     (Resolution on 2nd and 3rd premises, generating two new clauses)
4. {¬P, Q}, Q           (Resolution on 1st and 3rd premises, generating a new clause)
5. {¬P}                (Resolution on 4th and 3rd premises, generating a new clause)

Since we have obtained an empty clause in step 5, we can conclude that {P ⇒ Q} |= ((P ⇒ ¬Q) ⇒ ¬P).

Therefore, the provided formula is valid under the given premises.
What does the CNF mean in the last example ?

CNF stands for Conjunctive Normal Form. It is a standard form of representing logical formulas in propositional logic. A formula is in CNF if it is a conjunction of one or more clauses, where each clause is a disjunction of literals. A literal is a propositional variable or its negation.

For example, the CNF representation of the formula (P ∧ Q) ∨ (¬P ∧ R) is:
(P ∨ ¬P) ∧ (P ∨ R) ∧ (Q ∨ ¬P) ∧ (Q ∨ R)

Obtaining the CNF representation of a formula is often a necessary step in applying automated theorem-proving techniques, such as resolution or Davis-Putnam algorithm. This is because many algorithms work particularly well with formulas in CNF.

In the last example, we converted the negated conclusion of the original formula to CNF. This allowed us to apply the resolution rule to the premises and obtain a proof of the validity of the formula.

AD : φ
(φ ∨ ψ)

To prove (φ ∨ ψ) from the assumption AD : φ, we can use the rule of disjunction introduction. It states that if we have a formula φ, then we can derive the formula (φ ∨ ψ) for any formula ψ. Here's the proof:

1. AD: φ     (Assumption)
2. (φ ∨ ψ)  (Disjunction Introduction on line 1)

Therefore, we have proved that (φ ∨ ψ) follows from the assumption AD: φ.

What does it mean 
AD : φ
(φ ∨ ψ)

AD stands for "assumption" or "assumed proposition". In the context of a proof, it means that we are assuming φ to be true. 

The second line (φ ∨ ψ) is an inference or conclusion that follows from the assumption AD: φ. It means that either φ or ψ (or both) is true, since we know that φ is true by assumption. 

Overall, this notation represents a step in a proof, where we use an assumption to derive a new formula, which follows from the assumption using a logical rule (in this case, the rule of disjunction introduction).

What does it mean
AD : φ
_______
(φ ∨ ψ)

In the context of a proof, AD stands for "assumption" or "assumed proposition". 

The notation AD: φ at the beginning of the proof means that we are assuming φ to be true. 

The line below AD: φ with only a line above it represents a line of the proof, which doesn't have any formula written on it. This means that there are no premises or assumptions required to prove the formula on this line. 

The line (φ ∨ ψ) below the line with only a line above it represents the formula we are trying to prove. It means that it is true that either φ or ψ (or both) is true, since we know that φ is true by assumption.

Overall, this notation represents a simple proof which derives the disjunction (φ ∨ ψ) from the assumption that φ is true.

Using the FOPL system of natural deduction introduced in class, prove the following.
a) ` (¬∃xP(x)) ⇔ ∀x(¬P(x))

We will prove `(¬∃xP(x)) ⇔ ∀x(¬P(x))` using natural deduction. 

First, we will prove the forward direction: `(¬∃xP(x)) ⇒ ∀x(¬P(x))`

```
1. ¬∃xP(x)            (Assumption)
2. | assume P(a)      (Assumption)
3. | ∃xP(x)           (Existential Introduction with line 2)
4. | ⊥               (Contradiction with lines 1 and 3)
5. ¬P(a)             (Negation Introduction with lines 2-4)
6. ∀x(¬P(x))         (Universal Introduction with line 5)
```

In this proof, we assume the antecedent of the conditional and then use a proof by contradiction to derive the consequent. The assumption in line 2 introduces a new arbitrary constant, `a`, which we use to apply the existential introduction rule on line 3. We derive a contradiction on line 4 by using the negation of the existential quantifier in line 1 and the assumption of P(a) on line 2. This proves that if `(¬∃xP(x))` is true, then `∀x(¬P(x))` must also be true.

Next, we will prove the reverse direction: `∀x(¬P(x)) ⇒ (¬∃xP(x))`

```
1. ∀x(¬P(x))           (Assumption)
2. | assume ∃xP(x)     (Assumption)
3. | | assume P(a)     (Assumption)
4. | | ¬P(a)           (Universal Elimination with line 1)
5. | | ⊥               (Contradiction with lines 3 and 4)
6. | ¬P(a)             (Negation Introduction with lines 3-5)
7. | ∃xP(x) ⇒ ⊥        (Implication Introduction with lines 3-6)
8. | ⊥                 (Contradiction with lines 2 and 7)
9. ¬∃xP(x)             (Negation Introduction with lines 2-8)
```

In this proof, we assume the premise of the conditional and use a proof by contradiction to derive the negation of the antecedent. The assumption in line 2 introduces a new arbitrary constant, `a`, which we use to assume P(a) on line 3. We derive a contradiction on line 5 by using the universal quantifier in line 1 and the assumption of P(a) on line 3. We then use the negation of P(a) on line 6 to derive the implication `∃xP(x) ⇒ ⊥` on line 7, which is then contradicted on line 8 with the assumption in line 2. This proves that if `∀x(¬P(x))` is true, then `(¬∃xP(x))` must also be true.

Since we have proved both directions, we can conclude that `(¬∃xP(x)) ⇔ ∀x(¬P(x))` is true.

Using the FOPL system of natural deduction introduced in class, prove the following.
a) |- (¬∃xP(x)) ⇔ ∀x(¬P(x))

To prove that `|- (¬∃xP(x)) ⇔ ∀x(¬P(x))`, we need to prove that both `(¬∃xP(x)) ⇒ ∀x(¬P(x))` and `∀x(¬P(x)) ⇒ (¬∃xP(x))` are provable from no assumptions. 

First, we will prove `(¬∃xP(x)) ⇒ ∀x(¬P(x))`:

```
1. ¬∃xP(x)           (Assumption)
2. | assume P(a)     (Assumption)
3. | ∃xP(x)          (∃-Introduction with line 2)
4. | ⊥              (Contradiction with lines 1 and 3)
5. | ¬P(a)           (Negation Introduction with lines 2-4)
6. ∀x(¬P(x))        (∀-Introduction with line 5)
```

Second, we will prove `∀x(¬P(x)) ⇒ (¬∃xP(x))`:

```
1. ∀x(¬P(x))            (Assumption)
2. | assume ∃xP(x)      (Assumption)
3. | | assume P(a)      (Assumption)
4. | | ¬P(a)            (Universal Elimination with line 1)
5. | | ⊥                (Contradiction with lines 3 and 4)
6. | ¬P(a)              (Negation Introduction with lines 3-5)
7. | ∃xP(x) ⇒ ⊥         (∃-Elimination with lines 2-6)
8. | ⊥                  (Contradiction with lines 2 and 7)
9. ¬∃xP(x)              (Negation Introduction with lines 2-8)
```

Since we have proven both `(¬∃xP(x)) ⇒ ∀x(¬P(x))` and `∀x(¬P(x)) ⇒ (¬∃xP(x))`, we can conclude that `|- (¬∃xP(x)) ⇔ ∀x(¬P(x))`.

Using the tableau method introduced in class, check whether
a) |= ∃x(P(x) ∨ Q(x)) ⇔ (∃xP(x) ∨ ∃xQ(x))

We can use the tableau method to check whether `|= ∃x(P(x) ∨ Q(x)) ⇔ (∃xP(x) ∨ ∃xQ(x))`.

Starting from the left-hand side of the biconditional:

```
1. | ¬(∃x(P(x) ∨ Q(x)))             (Assumption)
2. | | ¬∃x(P(x) ∨ Q(x))            (Negation Introduction with line 1)
3. | | | ∀x¬(P(x) ∨ Q(x))          (Universal Introduction with line 2)
4. | | | | ¬(P(a) ∨ Q(a))          (Universal Elimination with line 3)
5. | | | | | ¬P(a) ∧ ¬Q(a)         (De Morgan's Law with line 4)
6. | | | | | ¬P(a)                 (Conjunction Elimination with line 5)
7. | | | | | ¬Q(a)                 (Conjunction Elimination with line 5)
8. | | | | ∃x¬P(x)                 (∃-Introduction with line 6)
9. | | | | ∃x¬Q(x)                 (∃-Introduction with line 7)
10.| | | ¬∃xP(x) ∧ ¬∃xQ(x)         (∧-Introduction with lines 8 and 9)
11.| | ¬(∃x(P(x) ∨ Q(x))) ⇒ (¬∃xP(x) ∧ ¬∃xQ(x)) (Implication Introduction with line 2 and 10)
```

Moving to the right-hand side of the biconditional:

```
12.| ¬(∃xP(x) ∨ ∃xQ(x))           (Assumption)
13.| | ¬∃xP(x)                     (Assumption)
14.| | | ∀x¬P(x)                   (Universal Introduction with line 13)
15.| | ∃x(P(x) ∨ Q(x))             (∃-Introduction with line 14)
16.| | | P(b) ∨ Q(b)               (Existential Elimination with line 15)
17.| | | | assume ¬P(b)            (Assumption)
18.| | | | ¬(P(b) ∨ Q(b))          (Universal Elimination with line 14)
19.| | | | ⊥                       (Contradiction with lines 16 and 17)
20.| | | P(b)                      (Negation Introduction with lines 17-19)
21.| | ∃xP(x)                      (∃-Introduction with line 20)
22.| | ¬¬∃xP(x)                    (Double Negation Introduction with line 21)
23.| | (∃xP(x)) ⇒ (∃xP(x) ∨ ∃xQ(x)) (Implication Introduction with line 22)
24.| ¬(∃xP(x) ∨ ∃xQ(x)) ⇒ ¬∃xP(x) (Implication Introduction with line 13 and line 23)
25.| | ¬∃xQ(x)                    (Assumption)
26.| | | ∀x¬Q(x)                  (Universal Introduction with line 25)
27.| | ∃x(P(x) ∨ Q(x))            (∃-Introduction with line 26)
28.| | | P(c) ∨ Q(c)              (Existential Elimination with line 27)
29.| | | | assume ¬Q(c)           (Assumption)
30.| | | | ¬(P(c) ∨ Q(c))         (Universal Elimination with line 26)
31.| | | | ⊥                      (Contradiction with lines 28 and 29)
32.| | | P(c)                     (Negation Introduction with lines 29-31)
33.| | ∃xP(x)                     (∃-Introduction with line 32)
34.| | ¬∃xP(x) ∧ ¬∃xQ(x)          (∧-Introduction with lines 25 and 33)
35.| ¬(∃xP(x) ∨ ∃xQ(x)) ⇒ (¬∃xP(x) ∧ ¬∃xQ(x)) (Implication Introduction with line 13 and line 34)
```

Since we have obtained the same formula (¬(∃xP(x) ∨ ∃xQ(x)) ⇒ (¬∃xP(x) ∧ ¬∃xQ(x))) in steps 11 and 35, and since it doesn't contain any open branches, we can conclude that `|= ∃x(P(x) ∨ Q(x)) ⇔ (∃xP(x) ∨ ∃xQ(x))`. 

Therefore, the biconditional is valid.
